query: Why is v-model syntax sugar in Vue?

competitors.label: Component v-model
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical Vue guide for component v-model expansion, modelValue/update:modelValue, arguments, and modifiers.
theirStrengths: The official guide is authoritative on the exact expansion of v-model for native inputs, custom components, arguments, and modifiers.
theirGaps: It is more reference-oriented than interview-compressed on the idea that v-model is just prop binding plus event listening.

realWorldUseCases: it doesn’t add a new runtime “two-way binding engine”.
So it’s just a shorter way to write “bind value + update on event”.
followUpConfusion: Arguments just swap the prop/event names.
Modifiers don’t change the “prop + event” structure

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain expansion accurately, but our page is stronger on the mental model that v-model is compile-time sugar rather than a magical two-way binding subsystem.
actionableExamples: The official examples are comprehensive, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely debugging follow-up: custom component bindings fail when one side of the prop-plus-event contract is missing.

actionableExamples: Keep one short checkbox or select expansion visible so the per-control property and event differences stay concrete.

competitors.winReason: The official guide is authoritative on the API, but our page is stronger for interview use because it frames v-model as a compiler rewrite and then explains the exact debugging contract that falls out of that.

competitors.label: Vue Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's Vue interview bank is directly relevant because v-model, component communication, and template syntax are core Vue interview fundamentals.
theirStrengths: It is a strong interview-prep hub for Vue bindings, forms, and component communication topics.
theirGaps: The category page is broader than this exact v-model expansion and debugging contract.

realWorldUseCases: This is the under-the-hood answer interviewers use to debug custom component bindings.
followUpConfusion: For components, v-model becomes a normal prop + a normal emitted event.
Arguments just swap the prop/event names. This enables multiple v-model bindings on one component.

realWorldUseCases: The bank is clearly relevant for Vue fundamentals, but it is less focused on the exact compile-time expansion story behind v-model.
actionableExamples: GreatFrontEnd remains competitive on example-driven interview practice, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: The landing page is less explicit about arguments, modifiers, and why modelValue plus update:modelValue is just a named prop-event pair.

actionableExamples: Keep the custom-component long form short so the page stays trivia-sized instead of becoming a full forms guide.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is useful as a Vue interview hub, but our page is stronger because it answers the exact 'what does v-model expand to?' question directly.

competitors.label: V Model Guide
competitors.selectionReason: LearnVue has a topic-direct article on v-model, including the prop-plus-event expansion for custom components.
theirStrengths: It is directly focused on how v-model works with custom components and shows the long-form expansion clearly.
theirGaps: It is lighter on arguments, modifiers, and the broader interview framing around syntax sugar.

realWorldUseCases: it’s compiled into a prop binding + an event listener.
followUpConfusion: For components in Vue 3 it maps to modelValue + update:modelValue

realWorldUseCases: The article is strong on custom component bindings, but our page is stronger on the broader under-the-hood explanation that covers native controls, arguments, and modifiers together.
actionableExamples: Its custom-component examples are strong, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that modifiers change value handling or event timing without changing the underlying prop-plus-listener shape.

actionableExamples: If needed later, add one compact multiple-v-model example to make argument-based expansion even more visible.

competitors.winReason: LearnVue is a strong direct comparator, but our page is stronger because it covers the full expansion story instead of only the basic custom-input case.
